title: Ipomoea serpyllifolia
intro: Ipomoea serpyllifolia, also known as Convolvulus serpyllifolius or small-leaved morning glory, is a climbing perennial plant native to tropical Asia, characterized by its small, oval-shaped leaves and tiny white or blue-purple flowers. The plant has been traditionally used in medicine, particularly in Ayurvedic and Unani systems, to treat various health issues, including fever, inflammation, and respiratory problems, due to its purported health benefits such as anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Ipomoea serpyllifolia include flavonoids, alkaloids, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional medicine, the plant is commonly made into herbal preparations, including decoctions, infusions, and powders, often used to treat coughs, colds, and other respiratory conditions.
taxonomy: Ipomoea serpyllifolia is a species of morning glory plant. It belongs to the family Convolvulaceae. This family is a group of flowering plants that includes around 1,800 species. Ipomoea serpyllifolia is a part of the genus Ipomoea, which comprises around 600 species of morning glories. The classification of Ipomoea serpyllifolia is a way to understand its evolutionary relationships and characteristics within the plant kingdom.
